run.output: Program executed without crashing; execution time: 3141.43 ms

validation.comparison_result: FAILED: row count mismatch — expected 10, got 0. Generated query produced empty result set (only CSV header written, zero data rows).

profiling.notes: High page fault rate indicates I/O-bound behavior with full file reads. No major faults, so no disk I/O stalls observed.

Q3.notes: Query produced no output rows; expected 10 rows with high revenue values from orders placed before 1995-03-15

Q3.bottleneck: CORRECTNESS FAILURE: Filter logic bug on l_shipdate predicate; secondary bug in GROUP BY key encoding
Q3.root_cause: Line 291 uses incorrect comparison operator (<=) instead of (>). SQL requires l_shipdate > '1995-03-15', but code filters with decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate, which INVERTS the result set. Combined with issue on line 306 where GROUP key encoding is lossy (missing o_orderdate), the query produces no results.
detailed_analysis: PRIMARY BUG (Line 291): The filter condition 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate) continue;' SKIPS all rows where l_shipdate <= 1995-03-15. But SQL says 'AND l_shipdate > DATE 1995-03-15', meaning we want shipdate AFTER the threshold. This inverts the logic completely.

SECONDARY BUG (Line 306): Group key encoding is `((int64_t)l_orderkey << 32) | (o_shippriority & 0xFFFF)` — this encoding LOSES the o_orderdate field (which is needed for the GROUP BY clause). The query should group by (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority), but the key only includes orderkey and shippriority.
IMPACT: No lineitem rows pass the shipdate filter (due to inverted logic), so global_agg hash table remains empty. select_topk(empty_agg, 10) returns empty vector, output CSV has only headers.

EXPECTED FLOW: Filter customer (c_mktsegment='BUILDING') → filter orders (o_orderdate < 1995-03-15) → join on custkey → scan lineitem, filter (l_shipdate > 1995-03-15) → join on orderkey → aggregate with 3-way GROUP BY → TOP-K 10 by revenue DESC.

analysis.overall: CRITICAL CORRECTNESS FAILURE. Query returns 0 rows instead of 10. Root cause is inverted filter logic on line 291 (uses <= when SQL requires >) combined with lossy GROUP BY key encoding on line 306. The generated code does not correctly implement the SQL semantics. Must fix both bugs before proceeding with performance optimizations.

critical_fixes.issue: Line 291: Inverted filter logic on l_shipdate — skips future shipments instead of past ones
critical_fixes.fix: Change 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate) continue;' to 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate) continue;' — ACTUALLY, REVERSE: keep records where l_shipdate > threshold. Change to: 'if (!(decoded_shipdate[i] > threshold_shipdate)) continue;' or equivalently 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate) continue;' is WRONG — should be 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] > threshold_shipdate) { /* process */ }'
critical_fixes.description: The lineitem scan filter must KEEP rows where l_shipdate > DATE '1995-03-15'. Currently line 291 inverts this by skipping (continuing/skipping) when decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate. CORRECT: Remove the 'continue' or swap the condition. The fixed line should be: 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate) continue;' should become 'if (!(decoded_shipdate[i] > threshold_shipdate)) continue;' — meaning skip if NOT greater than threshold. Or simply invert: keep only records passing the filter. NOTE: Re-reading line 291, it says 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate) continue;' which SKIPS records where shipdate is <= threshold. This is INVERTED from SQL 'l_shipdate > threshold'. The correct condition should process records where shipdate > threshold, not skip them. ACTUAL FIX: Remove the entire 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] <= threshold_shipdate) continue;' or change to 'if (decoded_shipdate[i] > threshold_shipdate) { /* process rest */ }'.
critical_fixes.actual_issue: Line 291 uses <= which means 'if l_shipdate is less than or equal to threshold, skip this row'. But the query wants l_shipdate > threshold. This is backwards. Should skip when l_shipdate <= threshold is actually CORRECT IF the rest of the row processing is in the if-block, but it's not — the 'continue' exits early, so it skips the entire processing of that row. The filter should be inverted: keep rows where l_shipdate > threshold (i.e., remove the continue or flip the condition to >).

critical_fixes.issue: Line 306: Group BY key encoding loses o_orderdate information
critical_fixes.fix: Expand the 64-bit group key encoding to include all three GROUP BY columns: l_orderkey (32-bit), o_orderdate (16-bit), o_shippriority (16-bit). Use: 'int64_t group_key = ((int64_t)l_orderkey << 32) | ((int32_t)o_orderdate << 16) | (o_shippriority & 0xFFFF);' to preserve date information (shifted into the middle 16 bits).
critical_fixes.description: The SQL query groups by (l_orderkey, o_orderdate, o_shippriority), but line 306 encodes the key as only '((int64_t)l_orderkey << 32) | (o_shippriority & 0xFFFF)', completely omitting o_orderdate. This causes multiple rows with the same (orderkey, shippriority) but different order dates to be merged incorrectly. The fix: Use all 64 bits of int64_t to encode all three fields. Current encoding: [orderkey:32] [free:32]. Better: [orderkey:32] [orderdate:16] [shippriority:16]. Note: o_orderdate is delta-encoded in storage but decoded as int32_t; dates from 1970-1995 fit in ~15 bits. o_shippriority is 0-3 (2 bits). Proposed: '((int64_t)l_orderkey << 32) | ((int32_t)o_orderdate & 0xFFFF) << 16 | (o_shippriority & 0xFFFF)'.

performance_optimizations.description: Current code processes all ~60M lineitem rows in a single thread. Hardware has 64 cores. Morsel-driven parallel execution across threads would reduce wall-clock time by up to 64x. Implement parallel lineitem scan with thread-local aggregation: divide lineitem into 64 morsels (~1M rows each), launch worker threads, each thread scans its morsel and builds a thread-local hash table, then merge all hash tables. See knowledge base: parallelism/thread-parallelism.md.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: From 3141ms → ~50-100ms (accounting for merge overhead). Target DuckDB's Q3 time: 77-112ms (SF10). GenDB has advantage of columnar binary storage (no parsing), so 50-100ms is reasonable.

performance_optimizations.technique: Avoid redundant full-table scans; use zone maps for predicate pushdown
performance_optimizations.description: Current code reads entire l_shipdate column (~60M rows, delta-encoded ~240MB). With zone maps on l_shipdate per 128K-row block, can skip blocks where all shipdate values <= 1995-03-15. Similarly, orders table is sorted by o_orderdate; can binary search to find the boundary (33% selectivity means ~5M qualifying rows). Implement zone map filtering: read zone map metadata, skip blocks not matching predicate, only decode/scan qualifying blocks. Reference storage_design.json shows 'idx_l_shipdate_zonemap' and 'idx_o_orderdate_zonemap' are available.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Reduces I/O by ~50-70% (estimating 67% of lineitem blocks are skippable if shipdate has uniform distribution). Wall-clock improvement depends on I/O vs CPU ratio; on HDD storage, ~20-30% speedup.

performance_optimizations.technique: Use compact hash tables (absl::flat_hash_map or folly::F14HashMap) instead of std::unordered_map
performance_optimizations.description: Lines 229, 281, 287 use std::unordered_map which has poor cache locality and ~60-70% load factor. Absl FlatHashMap uses dense packing (80%+ load factor) with SIMD-accelerated lookups. For Q3: customer hash table (~330K entries, built once), orders hash table (~5M entries after filtering, ~6.7M entries pre-filter), lineitem aggregation hash table (~100K entries estimated). Each lookup in std::unordered_map is ~3-5 CPU cycles with cache misses; FlatHashMap is ~1-2 cycles. Speedup: ~2-3x on hash lookup-heavy code. Reference: external-libs/abseil-folly.md, data-structures/compact-hash-tables.md.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Hash join and aggregation overhead reduced by 40-60%. Estimated wall-clock improvement after fixing correctness and parallelizing: ~10-15% (assuming join/agg is 20-25% of total time post-parallelization).

performance_optimizations.technique: Reorder joins: Customer → Orders → Lineitem is suboptimal; consider Orders → Customer → Lineitem or Lineitem → Orders → Customer
performance_optimizations.description: Current plan: (1) Filter customer by mktsegment (1.5M → ~330K rows); (2) Filter orders by orderdate (15M → ~5M rows); (3) Join orders+customer on custkey (5M probe, 330K build); (4) Scan+filter lineitem (60M rows), join with orders on orderkey (60M probe, 5M build). Join selectivity is high (FK constraint). Investigate whether scanning lineitem first, then joining with filtered orders, then joining with filtered customer is faster (probe lineitem first on smallest build table). Intermediate materialization cost: lineitem→orders join produces ~60M rows (FK), then customer join filters to ~6-8M. Current approach: customer→orders join produces ~5M rows, then lineitem join produces ~6-8M. Both routes produce similar intermediate cardinality; ordering may affect cache efficiency. Reference: joins/join-ordering.md.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: Join reordering unlikely to provide >10% improvement if cardinalities are similar. Main win is avoiding redundant hash table probes (measure current CPU time for join vs scan/agg).

performance_optimizations.technique: Implement two-phase partial aggregation for parallel execution
performance_optimizations.description: After fixing the group key encoding, implement thread-local aggregation for each morsel, then a global merge phase. Current code (line 287-325) already does local aggregation but in a single thread. Extend to parallel: each worker thread aggregates its lineitem morsel locally (no locks), then coordinator thread merges all local aggregation tables (locking is only during final merge, not per-row). This eliminates lock contention and reduces synchronization overhead. Reference: aggregation/partial-aggregation.md.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: When combined with thread parallelism, reduces merge overhead from O(n) to O(k) where k is number of threads (typically 64). Speedup: ~5-10% additional over base parallelism.

performance_optimizations.description: Lines 289-303 perform scalar filtering and revenue computation. Revenue computation: 'revenue = extendedprices[i] * (1.0 - discounts[i])' can be vectorized (AVX-512 on this hardware supports 8× double operations per cycle). Filter on l_shipdate can use SIMD comparison masks. Process 8 rows at a time. Reference: parallelism/simd.md. After multi-threading, SIMD becomes secondary optimization (thread-level parallelism dominates), but still useful for single-morsel performance.
performance_optimizations.expected_improvement: If single-morsel (1M rows) aggregation is CPU-bound, SIMD can provide 4-8x speedup on filtering+computation loop. Post-parallelization, benefit is ~1-2% since computation is already distributed across cores.

summary: CRITICAL: Q3 iteration 0 fails validation with 0 output rows (expected 10). Root causes: (1) Line 291 has inverted filter logic on l_shipdate (uses <= when SQL requires >), causing all lineitem rows to be filtered out; (2) Line 306 loses o_orderdate in GROUP BY key encoding. These semantic bugs MUST be fixed before optimization. Once correctness is restored, primary optimization opportunities are: (1) Parallelize across 64 cores (expected 50-100ms from current 3141ms), (2) Use zone maps to skip blocks (20-30% I/O reduction), (3) Upgrade to compact hash tables (2-3x lookup speedup). Current GenDB time 386ms (from benchmark) is much faster than current iter_0 failure; indicates previous iterations had different logic. After fixes + parallelism, expect to match or beat DuckDB's 77-112ms.
